title: Exercises: Future Directions in Quantum Chemistry
description: Explore emerging technologies and career planning

exercises.title: Quantum Computing Readiness
exercises.description: Evaluate a chemistry problem for quantum computing suitability.
exercises.task:
Consider these three chemistry problems:

1. Fe₄S₄ cluster in nitrogenase (iron-sulfur cofactor)
   - Strongly correlated, ~100 electrons
   - Classical: CASSCF limited, DMRG difficult

2. Vitamin C solvation energy
   - 176 atoms total
   - Weakly correlated, DFT accurate

3. Bond breaking in H₂
   - 2 electrons
   - Already solved classically

(a) Which problem could most benefit from quantum computing?
(b) Estimate qubit requirements for each (roughly)
(c) When might quantum advantage be achieved for each?

solution.part_a: Problem 1 (Fe₄S₄ cluster) would most benefit from quantum computing. It involves strong static correlation from multiple metal centers with partially filled d-orbitals. Classical methods struggle with this multi-reference character. Problem 2 can be solved with DFT (single-reference, weak correlation). Problem 3 is already solved easily classically.
solution.part_b: Qubit estimates (logical qubits for chemical accuracy): Problem 1: ~100-200 logical qubits (full active space of metal 3d and sulfur 3p orbitals). Problem 2: Not a good quantum target - would need 1000s of qubits, but classical methods work fine. Problem 3: 4 qubits sufficient (demonstrated on current hardware).
solution.part_c: Timeline estimates: Problem 3: Already demonstrated (2019 onwards). Problem 1: 2030+ with fault-tolerant quantum computers. This is the exciting target. Problem 2: Never advantageous for quantum - classical methods are sufficient and faster. Quantum computing targets should be problems where classical methods fundamentally struggle, not just large systems.

hints: Quantum advantage is for strongly correlated problems
Qubit count roughly equals active orbital count
Not all chemistry problems need quantum computers

exercises.title: ML Strategy Design
exercises.description: Design an ML strategy for a drug discovery problem.
exercises.task:
Your team needs to predict binding affinity for a kinase target across 1 million compounds.

Resources available:
- 500 experimental IC50 values for this target
- DFT/docking calculated for 10,000 compounds (5 CPU-hours each)
- 1000 CPU-hours/week budget
- 3 month timeline

(a) Design a multi-stage ML pipeline
(b) What ML architectures would you consider?
(c) How would you validate the final model?
(d) Estimate how many compounds you can experimentally test

solution.part_a: Multi-stage pipeline: Stage 1 (Week 1-2): Train initial model on 500 experimental + 10,000 DFT compounds. Use transfer learning from pre-trained chemistry models. Stage 2 (Week 3-6): Active learning - use uncertainty to select 200 compounds for new DFT calculations (200 × 5 = 1000 hrs). Retrain model. Stage 3 (Week 7-10): Second active learning round, add 200 more DFT. Stage 4 (Week 11-12): Final model, screen 1 million, select top 1000 for experimental validation.
solution.part_b: ML architectures: 1) Graph Neural Network (GNN) - SchNet, AttentiveFP for molecular graphs. 2) Ensemble of fingerprint models (RF, XGBoost) for interpretability. 3) Pre-trained ChemBERTa for SMILES-based features. 4) Combination: GNN for predictions + fingerprint models for applicability domain. Use uncertainty quantification (dropout, ensemble variance).
solution.part_c: Validation strategy: 1) Hold out 20% of experimental data as test set (never used in training). 2) Cross-validation on remaining 80% during development. 3) Time-split validation if data has temporal ordering. 4) Applicability domain analysis - predictions outside domain flagged. 5) Prospective validation: experimentally test top 50 predictions plus random sample.
solution.part_d: Experimental testing: Given typical kinase assay costs of ~$50-100/compound, with a reasonable budget of $100K, could test ~1000-2000 compounds. Select: top 500 by predicted affinity, top 100 by uncertainty (active learning), top 200 structurally diverse, 200 random baseline.

hints: Start with available data, add strategically

exercises.title: Self-Driving Lab Design
exercises.description: Design a self-driving lab for catalyst optimization.
exercises.task:
Design a self-driving laboratory system for optimizing heterogeneous catalysts for CO₂ reduction.

Components needed:
- Catalyst synthesis module
- Testing/characterization module
- Computational prediction module
- Decision-making AI

(a) Describe the closed-loop workflow
(b) What computations would guide the AI decisions?
(c) What experimental measurements are needed?
(d) How would you handle multi-objective optimization?

solution.part_a: Closed-loop workflow: 1) AI proposes catalyst composition and synthesis conditions. 2) Robotic synthesis: automated wet chemistry or sputtering. 3) Characterization: XRD for structure, XPS for oxidation states, BET for surface area. 4) Testing: Electrochemical CO₂RR in flow cell, measure current density, Faradaic efficiency, product distribution. 5) Data uploaded to central database. 6) ML model updated with new data. 7) Bayesian optimization proposes next experiment. 8) Repeat.
solution.part_b: Computational predictions: 1) DFT for surface adsorption energies (E_CO, E_H) - descriptor for activity. 2) ML model trained on previous experiments - predict activity/selectivity. 3) Stability prediction from formation energies. 4) Uncertainty quantification for exploration. 5) Descriptor-based catalyst screening using volcano relationships.
solution.part_c: Experimental measurements: Activity: Current density at fixed potential (mA/cm²). Selectivity: Faradaic efficiency for CO, HCOOH, CH₄, C₂H₄ (GC analysis). Stability: Performance over 24hr operation. Structure: XRD, TEM, EXAFS. Composition: ICP-OES, XPS.
solution.part_d: Multi-objective optimization: Use Pareto optimization for: Max CO selectivity, Max current density, Max stability, Min cost (avoid rare elements). Bayesian multi-objective optimization (MOBO) to find Pareto front. Human-in-the-loop for selecting which trade-offs to explore. Constraints: Element availability, synthesis feasibility.

hints: Close the loop between experiment and computation

exercises.title: Career Planning
exercises.description: Plan your computational chemistry career development.
exercises.task:
Create a 5-year career development plan for computational chemistry.

Assume you have:
- Chemistry PhD with some computational experience
- Basic Python programming
- DFT experience (Gaussian)
- Interest in drug discovery

(a) Identify 3-5 skill gaps to address
(b) Create a learning plan for Years 1-2
(c) Define target position for Year 5
(d) Identify networking and visibility opportunities

solution.part_a: Skill gaps to address: 1) Machine Learning - limited experience with ML for chemistry. 2) Production programming - need stronger software engineering practices. 3) Free energy calculations - FEP/TI not yet mastered. 4) Structural biology - protein modeling experience needed. 5) Business/communication - present to non-computational audiences.
solution.part_b: Learning plan Years 1-2: Year 1 Q1-Q2: Online ML course (Coursera/fast.ai), practice on chemistry datasets. Year 1 Q3-Q4: Master FEP calculations (tutorials, small projects, join FEP benchmark consortium). Year 2 Q1-Q2: Learn molecular dynamics and protein modeling (MDAnalysis, PyMOL). Year 2 Q3-Q4: Contribute to open-source project (RDKit, OpenMM). Throughout: Read 2 papers/week, attend journal club, present at local seminars.
solution.part_c: Year 5 target position: Senior Computational Scientist at biotech/pharma. Responsibilities: Lead computational support for 2-3 drug discovery programs. Mentor 1-2 junior scientists. Develop and maintain ML models for binding affinity. Interface with medicinal chemistry, biology, and leadership. Publish 2-3 papers/year, present at ACS meetings.
solution.part_d: Networking and visibility: 1) Attend ACS COMP division meetings - present poster/talk. 2) Join OpenEye/Schrödinger user groups. 3) Contribute to RDKit or other open source. 4) Start LinkedIn technical posts monthly. 5) Attend local computational chemistry meetups. 6) Reach out to 2 people/month for informational interviews. 7) Consider industry externship or sabbatical.

hints: Be specific about skill gaps and learning methods
